focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

Nobody had slept.

By ten in the morning the bat lab smelled like rubbing alcohol, hot plastic, and the sweet black rot the mangroves breathed through every screen. Rhea's recorder sat on a folded dish towel between a tray of guano bags and a chipped mug full of Sharpies. Nothing about it looked official. Mina had listened to the file six times already. It was still mostly prop wash, spoonbill racket, the slap of Tomas correcting the boat against roots. Then came the wrongness inside it, a submerged whine that bent upward where no engine should have gone, and after that a wet blue smear in the video frame that made the hair on her forearms lift every time.

Rhea stood at the counter with her braid half undone and a strip of freezer tape stuck to the back of one wrist. She had not slept. It showed more in her precision than in her face. When she was tired enough, her movements got cleaner, almost ceremonial, cutting mess down to pieces she could stack.

"Play the thirty-second segment again," she said.

Mina dragged the cursor back. The laptop gave them the same bad miracle. Dark water, spoonbills shrieking overhead, Tomas saying, easy, easy, and then the brief electric turn under the surface. Not long enough. Not clean enough. Enough to make her chest go light anyway.

Rhea listened with one hand braced flat on the counter. "All right," she said. "Three separate problems."

"Only three. That's practically a grant proposal."

"Evidence integrity, access, and your name."

Mina leaned back in the plastic chair until it complained under her. "You always know how to sweet-talk a morning."

"I wasn't hired for morning charm." Rhea peeled the freezer tape from her wrist and stuck it to the recorder case. In her block handwriting she wrote ORIGINAL CARD, then look...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...sed it again.

Tomas picked up the map tube from the umbrella stand and knocked it once against his palm. "Next time," he said, and Mina looked up because the phrase itself felt like a reprieve she had not earned, "if we're going back into those cuts, I want every rotten piece of history on the table first. I don't care if it makes you look filthier. I don't care if it makes me sound stupid for being here. I want the whole damn creek, not the brochure."

Rhea said, "Before the next boat."

Tomas looked at her. "Before the next gallon."

Mina closed her hand around the hot mug until it hurt. Outside, the post-storm light was finally gathering into actual morning, turning the mangroves from one black mass into many separate black things. The camp creaked. The bucket under the roof seam rang again. Somewhere along the walkway Beto started whistling a song he never remembered all the words to, only the tune, and the tune kept losing arguments with a bent hinge. Farther out, beyond the mangroves, the rookery was getting loud again, all stolen sticks and bill-clatter.

No one reached for her. No one told her to go.

Tomas pulled the county map from its tube and snapped it open across the table, trapping one corner with his phone and another with the sugar jar. "Show me the cut."

Mina set down her mug. Coffee ringed the paper. She put two fingers on the south intake branch where the concrete ran under the roots.

Rhea came to her shoulder in Tomas's shirt, close enough for the damp cotton to brush Mina's arm, and stopped there. Tomas uncapped a carpenter's pencil with his teeth and boxed the channel where Mina's fingers had been.

When the screen door shut behind him, the map stayed open on the table, her route trapped in pencil between the sugar jar and Rhea's recorder case.
